sentence: Subjective:
The patient, a 37-year-old female, presents with a complaint of worsening headaches over the past few weeks.
She reports her blood pressure has been running high, with readings of 160/87 and 175/92 in the past three weeks.
She has a history of hypertension and diabetes, and has been taking lisinopril 20mg once a day, but admits to sometimes forgetting to take it, particularly on weekends.
She has also been experiencing occasional headaches, which she rates as a 7 out of 10 in intensity, and has been managing them with Tylenol.
She denies any recent changes in urination or other symptoms.
She reports a family history of migraines, but not similar headaches like the ones she is currently experiencing.
Past medical history:
The patient has a history of hypertension and diabetes.
She has been prescribed lisinopril 20mg once a day for hypertension.
Review of systems:
The patient denies any recent changes in urination, cough, runny nose, or congestion.
Current medications:
Lisinopril 20mg once a day for hypertension.
Objective:
Vital signs: Blood pressure 160/87 and 175/92, but previously was 124/82.
Physical exam:
The patient has tenderness to palpation over the frontal sinuses, but no tenderness over the maxillary sinuses or lymphadenopathy.
Test Results:
[NOT MENTIONED]
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient presents with worsening headaches and uncontrolled hypertension.
Problem list: Hypertension; Diabetes; Headache.
Plan:
I prescribed lisinopril 40mg once a day for hypertension.
I ordered blood tests, including hemoglobin A1c and other laboratory tests, to monitor her diabetes and hypertension.
I referred her to see Doctor Ruth for further evaluation and education on managing her diabetes.
I advised her to continue taking Tylenol for her headaches and to keep checking her blood pressure.
Follow-up:
I instructed the patient to return for follow-up after her blood tests are complete and to review the results with me or Doctor Ruth.
